1. A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sharp chest pain that worsens with inspiration and improves when leaning forward. He reports a low-grade fever for the past two days. On auscultation, a pericardial friction rub is heard. ECG shows diffuse ST elevation with PR depression. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
Pneumonia
Acute pericarditis
Pulmonary embolism
ST-elevation myocardial infarction
Aortic dissection
Acute pericarditis
Acute pericarditis is inflammation of the pericardial sac, most commonly caused by viral infection (e.g., Coxsackievirus). Classic features include pleuritic chest pain relieved by leaning forward, a pericardial friction rub, and diffuse ST elevation with PR depression on ECG.
(Choice A) Pneumonia typically presents with productive cough, fever, and lobar consolidation on chest X-ray, not a friction rub or PR depression.
(Choice C) Pulmonary embolism presents with pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, tachycardia, and risk factors such as immobility or DVT, without friction rub or diffuse ECG changes.
